speaker: interviewer
text: It's tape number 4, and we are interviewing Mr. Yehudah Bakon. Now, we are-- we finished our last tape when you spoke about painting-- your painting and your art, in Theresienstadt. Now I'd like to ask you, what did people say about your art? What was the impression that you got back-- the feedback?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a child, and it was a gifted child. It wasn't yet art. But, as I told you, my first time I sold something was for a very high price-- a half loaf of bread. But, as I was a gifted child, I got all the encouragement through Fredy Hirsch, through Jacob Edelstein. And of-- through them, I could meet all the artists who supported me with pencils, paper, and critique and teaching. And I was just drawing what I found interesting. And, of course, I was looking over the shoulders-- which was fascinating for me, to see a real artist. And when you are 13 and 1/2 or something like that, to see an artist at work, or to be even in his atelier, that was a kind of very exciting thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now do you remember any special even-- events that happened in Theresienstadt that you would like to tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Our teacher from Mahrisch Ostrau, who was the head teacher of the class-- headmaster of our class-- was Leopold Lowinger. And I knew him very well, and his son was also my good friend. And his wife was related to Sigmund Freud. And he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: To whom was she related?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sigmund Freud.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She came from Moravia-- Freud was born in Moravia, not far from Mahrisch Ostrau. And he was a librarian in Theresienstadt. In Theresienstadt was a library-- a lending library. By the way, later on I will show you a book. I have still a book, the Bible, with the stamp Zentral Ghetto Bucherei.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a story. [LAUGHS] I got it after the war, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that is from this library. Anyhow, this teacher was there in this library. And I remember, once I went to the [NON-ENGLISH] Kaserne, and I-- and there were a lot of people. And suddenly a little tractor driven by a German-- young SS man-- squeezes my teacher to the wall, and killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Why?

speaker: interviewer
text: Just like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just like that! And, of course, it was my teacher. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saw it. That was the beginning of very brutal things which I saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it the first time you saw someone killed brutally?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you saw dead people before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I saw a few dead people. And I, I know what is happening from the rumors of-- still in Mahrisch Ostrau-- of people who run away from Poland and told us all kinds of terrible things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, but you, yourself--

speaker: interviewee
text: By myself, it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see dead--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Theresienstadt, of course, we saw--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Theresienstadt, these-- the old people died.

speaker: interviewee
text: That we saw. But somebody killed in this brutal way-- that was the very first time. Because, altogether, in Theresienstadt, they were not mass killing. They-- not in my time, but, I think a little before, they hanged a few peoples because they found some letters they tried to smuggle out. But, as such, this SS man who went and sometimes didn't like somebody, who could kick him or hit him in his face, and so on, the usual thing. It wasn't out of normality. But really, killing in such a way, I only saw later in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see-- did-- were there many Germans walking around Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were not many. But next to us, there was a street-- as if a normal street-- which was close to us. But there were people-- German-- walking, and so on. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Soldiers-- German soldiers. SS.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mmm, soldiers, SS men. But in Theresienstadt altogether were not so many of them visible. There were always some Germans and a Czech policeman who were guarding the crossroad or where you couldn't easily cross from one street to another or checkpoints.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you exactly the number. But I know that, for, for a child, it was terribly overcrowded. And the condition for us were also pretty bad. But nothing to compare with the barracks where my father or my mother with my sister, lived or the worst places, where the old people were. It was very, very badly overcrowded.

speaker: interviewer
text: But altogether, there's-- is there a number that you could point out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- as a child, I didn't deal with kind of numbers. Today, we know there were about-- I don't know-- so and so many thousands. And I only--

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't know, exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think 120,000 went through, or I don't know exactly the numbers. How, how many went through, that, I can't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, because there were all the transport--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they were always coming and going. I remember, when we arrived with our still normal train, just Theresien-- a train for cattle was leaving Theresienstadt at the same time. And I remember this train, which was with these tiny little windows and barbed wire. And some very, very sad faces were looking out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they come from, these trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew that this train were people who were deported from Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Theresienstadt. And you saw them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saw them when we came into Theresienstadt. And later on, of course--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were, yourself. And when did it happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I arrived, in '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, but when did you, yourself, go on a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went in the winter '43. There were two transports. But before, something horrible happened to us, and that was the gathering. All of the Jews had to go to a valley which was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it an evacuation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We didn't know what it is. Counting-- they were suspecting that somebody is missing or so. And they count the whole population of Theresienstadt. That was in the late autumn of '43. And they-- it was called Bohusovice [? Kotlina, ?] the Valley of Bohusovice. And we went, all of us, in the early morning. And the whole, huge valley was filled up with all the people. In the beginning, young-- youngsters as us-- it was fun, even. But later on, we didn't know how to go to the toilet. We couldn't move. And then it went dark. And we were afraid they will shoot us or something will happen. And it was a terrible experience of fear. And counting-- endless counting. And only late at-- in the evening, when it was very dark, we were dispensed and sent back. Many of the elderly people died afterwards from pneumonia, and so on. And we were so lucky. It was like to come back to a home, because suddenly we knew what it mean-- meant to have a roof over your head. And whoever went through this experience didn't forget it. And at that time, the rumor was that something wasn't exact with the counting, and they put Edelstein into jail. And another man-- I think Epstein and Murmelstein-- they were the other Juden Alteste and [GERMAN]. Murmelstein was also somebody I knew through connection with Edelstein. He was a rabbi from Vienna-- not so sympathetic man-- a very fat man. And he spoke German and Austrian German. And he and Epstein, they were afterwards the Juden Alteste. But, as Edelstein couldn't any more help us, we were sent to a transport. I think it was DR and DS. It was two transport to the east. Each of them--

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was when?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in about the 15 of December, '43. And that was one of the most horrible experiences.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, actually, you didn't have the protection of Edelstein?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- of Edelstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because even Edelstein himself was in the same transport. But about Edelstein, only--

speaker: interviewer
text: But Edelstein did something that the Germans wanted to, to, to punish him, or to--

speaker: interviewee
text: To get rid of him or punished. So I knew Miriam, his wife, and his son. And I remember they prepared everything themselves for, for the transport, meaning, they wrote on all the valises the number of the transport, and so on. And it was very sad, because we didn't have any more-- any protection. Nobody could help us. Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: So they left before you? No, you left together.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they left together in the same transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you left with your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to leave everything--

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it? Did you get--

speaker: interviewee
text: We got a--

speaker: interviewer
text: --a notice that your--

speaker: interviewee
text: --a notice that I have to be at this-and-this time and hour with my belongings. And-- but this time we didn't went into a normal train, but to this kind of train of

speaker: interviewer
text: Cattle train.

speaker: interviewee
text: --cattle train--

speaker: interviewer
text: Together with mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Together with mother and sister and father. And my mother wasn't any more in very good health. And we were about 80 people horded in this train. We got, I remember, some, some water and a few loaves of bread meant for little children and old people. And the feeling when they sealed us, and they closed it-- it was like in, in a grave. And it was unforgettable because the little children cried, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people couldn't sit-- couldn't-- of course, I couldn't even lay down. We were somehow sitting and squeezing each other. And then the train-- I remember the last look on Theresienstadt. I could still see the symbol of Theresienstadt-- the little tower of the little church which was there. And towards the east, we passed Mahrisch Ostrau, which was somehow like in a Gothic story. And, later on when we came to the other place, Poland, they changed [INAUDIBLE] already the main train because suddenly I could hear the whistle was a different whistle. Because we were in a military-- near the front. And the whistle of the train was frightening. It is a different tune that I remember. And as long as we have been in Czechoslovakia-- at that time called Protektorat-- the whistle had the normal whistle we were used to. And suddenly we came to near the front into Poland--

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was probably a different--

speaker: interviewee
text: It had a different--

speaker: interviewer
text: --type of locomotive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Locomotive. But anyhow, the voice was horrifying. And we arrived at night in a place. I looked through. I could see a lot of reflectors, against--

speaker: interviewer
text: Lights, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --at night, and some huge balloons. I didn't know what it is. Later on, I knew that these hu-- like a very fat fishes. But they were antiaircraft balloons which were always put up, because nearby was Buna and some factories, and they were against--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you arrived in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we arrived in main Auschwitz. I didn't know it at the time, but it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it  Auschwitz, or Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz. And then, suddenly, I saw through when-- still it was terribly quiet, terribly quiet, like death. And I saw many, many people from far in dressed as soldiers or something like that-- military dress. And everyone had a stick in his hand. So I said, well, it must be a recreation home for soldiers or something like that because everyone had a stick in his hand. And I saw only, only these soldiers. But few minutes later, the wagon was opened, and the unhuman crying and shouting, and people with kind of dress I've never seen-- these strips, and with numbers sewn, and all kinds of signs, and screaming like-- not like human beings. Raus! Raus! Leave everything. Nothing you can take in. Off, off! Go, go, go,! Quick, quick, quick!

speaker: interviewer
text: And with sticks and pushing us out. And near-- These people with the stripes? Or are you speaking about the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah,  these people with the stripes, and nearby SS man who encouraged-- los, los, go on. And there were some huge they called it, DKW, Deutsche Kraft Wagen-- these huge military--

speaker: interviewer
text: Trucks.

speaker: interviewee
text: --trucks. And we were squeezed in the trucks. But so squeezed that I was afraid I will fell over. I was just on, on the border. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: The end of it, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: The end of it. But it was worse than sardines. And of course, later on I knew it was a miracle. But, because in Auschwitz, everyone who was put on this truck-- that was the symbol to going only to gas. And especially if you were so squeezed like that. And we went-- I don't know-- for 20 minutes it could take, or maybe longer. No voice-- nothing. Only landscape. And I saw points of light-- geometrically points of light-- I didn't know what it is. But very geometrically, only light, light, light, light. And, of course, later I knew it was the electric wires. And we went to Birkenau. And there was no sign.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was night?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was night. And always when there were new oncomers, there was a curfew. So you didn't hear a voice-- nothing. Only you are suddenly in a completely different world. And we came-- which we know later-- to B2B. It's a part of Birkenau, which was a couple of kilometers from Auschwitz I, where we arrived in the main station. And we came into a block. I remember I looked what it is. There was still written in German some-- I think that it said stable for horses, for so many and so many horses. And we were put just on, on this kind of-- into these bunkers, which were much more unpleasant than, than in Theresienstadt. It was a huge block, I think for, for 20 or so horses.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you came, it was empty, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was empty, and we were perched into it and thrown into it and deadly tired, after three days of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you were three days on the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Two or three nights it took us. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: But your parents were with you, and your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we were-- no, I think I came with my father-- sep-- they put men separately and women separately. And only next day, somehow, I could get some contact. And we were completely bewildered. And we asked, where are we? And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you ask?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the kapo-- the one who dealt with us, who was also a Czech. But he arrived a little earlier-- some, I think, four months or so-- in September, October, something like that. He arrived-- this transport, before us, arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came in December, yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came in December. And he said, well, you're in-- you're in Auschwitz. It's the end station. If you behave well, you have a chance to live a couple of months. Everyone goes up the chimney. And if you don't like it, here are barbed wire. You can throw yourself in the barbed wire. That was--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was told you on your arrival?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the arrival.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you also heard it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. And it was a completely unbelievable situation. Soon afterwards, they came and-- of course they took all our luggage, and we just came by ourself. That if somebody has rings or gold and, oh, I don't know, something still, he has to give it at once. If not, he will be shoot. At the greatest pleasure of the Blockalteste with somebody else were collecting-- whoever had, had to give it away. And then I remember the name of our Blockalteste was Bondi. And he was from the first transport. And somebody came to him and said, oh, Bon-- I don't know, let's say, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: How are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I am this-and-this one. So he gave him a tremendous smack and said, here I'm not your so-and-so. Here am I your blockalteste, and you have to greet me, and so you have to do this. And that, for a child, was a tremendous shock, that people could behave like this. One of us, even. The next day, we were gathered, and we had to go somewhere. We went to the sauna. We didn't know what it is, and so on. We marched out of the camp, in five-- always, everything five. It was always five people, in a row of five. But we children were with the very first.

speaker: interviewer
text: But women, too, or only men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Separately. I remember because we were in the block of men. We arrived-- we went first. And we went somewhere on a-- out of the camp. And there were some factory or something on the right side. And, because I was the very first one and the SS man was a little behind usm somehow we went in. And there was a big [INAUDIBLE]. And I looked around. There was a tiny, little window on the side. And I was just looking around-- what it is. And then he said, raus, raus! We should go out, and we should go some other way. And that was the gas chamber of crematorium number 4, which later on, I visited several times. But at that time I didn't know it, what it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you went there by mistake?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: The road--

speaker: interviewee
text: The road went there, and we had to go in another way. But the first group went into it. And he said raus, raus. But I could see it exactly how it looked, and with these windows, and so on-- these small windows on the top.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went further on it. We came to a huge place, which was called sauna. We didn't had idea what is sauna. Later, we knew about it. It's-- in Finnish or so, it means washing hall, a special-- Area, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --area. But our transport had special treatment that we only, of course, knew later. Usually, if a transport came, maybe we are the only-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] Tell it to you. So let's say I have a transport of 2,000 people. We were 2,500. There were two groups of 2,500 people. They were selected on the spot. And only few peoples--

speaker: interviewer
text: Went to the sauna.

speaker: interviewee
text: --went to the camps. All mothers and children till the age of 16 went automatically to the gas chamber. Everyone who was over, more or less, 40-- because in Auschwitz, that was more-- if you were 35, 40, you looked like a 61, and you were not anymore capable to work. You were sent automatically to the gas. So only this tiny, little group--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were actually in the age--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came with everyone-- and soon you will understand why-- with old people, children, and sick people. We went to the sauna. Now we came still with our clothes and everything. Now we had to take off our clothes and put it-- like if somebody comes to a prison, if you saw it in pictures, you had a huge bag, and you put all your belongings into the prison. We had to get naked. And then we could see what is going around. Maybe there was another transport, and so on. And I remember I saw-- and we had still some food with us, which we had in our pockets, because, you know, we were very clever where to put-- so we ate something. And some of this-- it was a ration. And must have been somebody with a longer time got hold of a piece of wurst-- of sausage. And he made a bite. And the SS man saw it. From us-- must have been from somebody with [INAUDIBLE]. Take it out! Take it out! And started to beat him savagely. But he preferred to be beaten but to have this one bite, and to swallow it, which I couldn't understand. He's beaten to-- blood was pouring. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was eating.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- just this little bite.

speaker: interviewer
text: This little.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And to us they behaved quite nice. We had to sign something. We are going voluntary to preventive custod-- custod-- preventive prison against the rest of the German people. We go voluntary. That, we had to sign. I mean, even that they took our clothes individually and put it in the sack was something. Of course, they didn't give us any receipt for our clothes, but at least they behaved to us in a different way than they usually

speaker: interviewer
text: Behaved.

speaker: interviewee
text: --behaved. Later on, we really came to a kind of sauna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And you washed?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we were-- all our hair on, on the-- our hair on the head, they left. Even to the women, and so on. But only the bodily hairs. And we were shaved. We were put into a terrible Lysol or some solution. So it's not so painful, but bearable. And afterwards we had to stay for an appell-- waiting.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were really washed? Did you get some water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some water, yes. We were wa-- kind of water. And we were waiting. I saw some installation for disinfection--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were naked? You had--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were naked. And we had-- in this winter, we had to wait-- sometimes inside, sometimes outside. The very old people, of course, collapsed, and some of them died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after many hours, we were put in another room. And there were heaps of clothe. And they distributed the clothes. I mean, of course it didn't fit at all. But everyone got something. Some piece of-- not a real [YIDDISH], not a real-- how you call it? Something to hold on you your trousers.

speaker: interviewee
text: But elderly people who could still hold their glasses, I remember. And when we looked at each other, we couldn't recognize each other, even. We were looking so strange. And for a child it was comic, even, how we looked. And I remember there was also a heap of gloves. And I took a special another pair of gloves because I thought about with my mother when she will need to go to work. And I managed to smuggle out another pair of gloves, next day, to give it to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I also remember that one elderly man went to SS man. Now I have to tell you that nobody dared to speak or go near to SS man. It was even forbidden. You are not allowed, in Auschwitz, to go near to SS man. But he went to him and said, like, mister, can I have aspirin? I have headache. SS said, of course, my dear. And he went and brought him aspirin. So it was a completely abnormal behavior of the SS towards us. Now we shall stop for a second. It's the end of the tape.
